children.value: You now have a grid of integers. This chapter asks what those integers represent — and reveals the first major reason they cannot be trusted as direct measurements of scene content.
4.1 Contrast — The Spread of Pixel Values

children.label: 4.1 Contrast — The Spread of Pixel Values

children.value: is how far apart the dark and bright pixels are. A high-contrast image uses the full 0–255 range. A low-contrast image clusters all its values in a narrow band.

children.html: C=Imax⁡−Imin⁡Imax⁡+Imin⁡C = \frac{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} + I_{\min}}C=Imax​+Imin​Imax​−Imin​​
Cσ=std(I)C_\sigma = \text{std}(I)Cσ​=std(I)

children.value: Both measure the same thing: how much the pixel values vary.
two images of the same scene at different contrast levels have different pixel values for every pixel — even though the content is identical. Pixel-level comparison treats them as different images.
When the same scene is photographed under different lighting, the pixel values are related by an

children.html: I2(i,j)=a⋅I1(i,j)+bI_2(i,j) = a \cdot I_1(i,j) + bI2​(i,j)=a⋅I1​(i,j)+b
aaa
bbb

children.value: This is not an approximation — it follows directly from the linear relationship between photon flux and electron count in the sensor (Chapter 2). Different exposure time, different aperture, or different ambient light all change

children.html: aaa
bbb

children.value: the same face photographed in morning light and afternoon light produces pixel values that differ by

children.html: aI+baI + baI+b

children.value: at every location. A naive pixel comparator sees two different faces.

The Gonzalez & Woods textbook shows the same portrait at three contrast levels. The pixel values differ by up to 100 grey levels between the low and high contrast versions — despite showing the same content.

children.alt: Einstein portrait at low, medium, and high contrast — same content, very different pixel values
Affine transform table — pixel value distributions under different a and b parameters

children.value: is the ratio between the brightest and darkest values the sensor can capture simultaneously:

children.html: DR=20log⁡10 ⁣(Imax⁡Imin⁡)(dB)\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{\max}}{I_{\min}}\right) \quad \text{(dB)}DR=20log10​(Imin​Imax​​)(dB)

children.value: Real-world scenes routinely exceed sensor dynamic range. A window in a dark room spans 20+ stops — more than any camera can capture in a single exposure. The sensor must clip one end.
When scene brightness exceeds the sensor’s range, pixel values are
pixels at the bright end saturate to 255. All detail in bright regions is permanently lost — white blobs.
pixels at the dark end round to 0. Detail in dark regions is lost — black blobs.

Clipping is irreversible. Unlike shot noise (which can be partially reduced by averaging) or quantization (which can be reduced by more bits), clipped values carry no information about the original brightness — the ADC has discarded it.
The implication for contrast stretching (a common pre-processing step): stretching a clipped image amplifies the clipping artefact. The white blob becomes a bigger white blob.
Spread of pixel values; same scene → different values at different contrast

children.html: I2=aI1+bI_2 = aI_1 + bI2​=aI1​+b

children.value: Values outside DR are saturated; information permanently lost

children.value: is the central obstacle of Chapters 6–7. Fixing it requires normalisation — but normalisation has a ceiling, which Part II explores.
Chapter 5 — Colour and the Imaging Pipeline
: so far we have treated images as grayscale. Real cameras capture colour — adding three more dimensions of variability to the pixel value problem.
